rulings.Case Title: IN RE APPLICATION OF: JOHN/JANE DOE
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Quash Deposition Subpoena Duces Tecum And For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, April 23, 2019, Line 1, PETITIONER JOHN/JANE DOE's Motion To Quash Deposition Subpoena Duces Tecum And For Monetary Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Paul Renne, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: NO OPPOSITION HAS BEEN FILED TO PLAINTIFF'S MOTION TO QUASH THE SUBPOENA ADDRESSED TO TWITTER, ACCORDINGLY, THE COURT WILL ISSUE AN ORDER QUASHING THE SUBPOENA AND IMPOSING MONETARY SANCTIONS IN THE AMOUNT OF $19,119.57, PAYMENT OF WHICH SHOULD BE MADE TO PLAINTIFF WITHIN TEN (10) DAYS FROM THE DATE OF THIS ORDER. COUNSEL FOR PLAINTIFF SHOULD PREPARE AN APPROPRAIATE ORDER, DELIVER A COPY TO DEFENDANT FOR APPROVAL AS TO FORM AND DELIVER A COPY TO THE COURT FOR SIGNING AT THE HEARING ON APRIL 23, 2019. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rennepar85@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: QIAN & NEMECEK LLP, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED LIABILITY VS. PETER M. RADIN JR.
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Qian & Nemecek Llp'S Motion To Compel Compliance By Laurel Spe, Llc With Subpoena Duces Tecum And For Sanctions In The Amount Of $10,365.41 (Added For Purposes Of Entering Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Plaintiff Qian & Nemecek LLP's Motion To Compel Compliance By Laurel Spe, LLC With Subpoena Duces Tecum And For Sanctions In The Amount Of $10,365.41 *** Tentative Ruling - Part 2 of 3 *** Second, Laurel SPE contends that the subpoena is of no force and effect because the attached affidavit is deficient under Code of Civil Procedure section 1985. Laurel SPE is wrong. The Discovery Act provides that a records and testimony subpoena "need not be accompanied by an affidavit or declaration showing good cause for the production." (Code Civ. Proc. § 2020.501(b); see Terry v. SLICO (2009) 175 Cal.App.4th 352, 359 [holding that § 2020.501(b) controls over contrary provisions in §§ 1985(b) and 1987.5 requiring affidavits showing good cause for production, and that deposition subpoena to nonparty witness was valid even though it had not been accompanied by an affidavit or declaration].) The parties disagree on the effect of Laurel SPE's failure to produce any documents responsive to the subpoena. Plaintiff contends that by failing to serving written objections to the subpoena within three days, Laurel SPE waived all objections to the documents sought by the subpoena. Plaintiff is incorrect: Code of Civil Procedure section 2025.410(a), on which it relies, applies only to "[a]ny party served with a deposition notice"; it does not apply to nonparties such as Laurel SPE. Laurel SPE, for its part, contends that it was not required to serve written objections or to move to quash the subpoena (or for a protective order). However, Laurel SPE mischaracterizes the holding in Monarch Healthcare v. Superior Court (2000) 78 Cal.App.4th 1282. That case held only that a nonparty served with a "records only" or "records and testimony" subpoena need not file a motion to quash the subpoena, but may "simply wait and object, putting the onus on the proponent to move to compel." (Id. at 1287.) The nonparty in Monarch Healthcare timely produced certain responsive documents and raised objections on trade secrets and privacy grounds to other documents sought in the subpoena. (Id. at 1285.) Here, in contrast, Laurel SPE never produced any responsive documents nor served any written objections to the subpoena, nor is there any evidence that it objected in any form or at any time to producing documents; it simply failed to respond. (Supp. Nemecek Decl. 5.) The parties are directed to meet and confer regarding any legitimate objections Laurel SPE may raise, on financial privacy or other grounds, to the scope of the subpoena. (See Code Civ. Proc. § 708.120; Fox Johns Lazar Pekin & Wexler, APC v. Superior Court (2013) 219 Cal.App.4th 1210, 1222 ["the examination of a third party under section 708.120 is limited to exploring the third party's possession of the judgment debtor's property or any debt owed the judgment debtor"],.) Laurel SPE shall produce responsive documents within 10 days of the Court's order. Plaintiff's motion pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 1987.2 for sanctions in the amount of $10,365.41 is denied. For the reasons set forth above, the Court finds that Laurel SPE opposed the motion without substantial justification. However, as set forth in the Court's March 12, 2019 Order, "law firms and attorney litigants are precluded from recovering attorney fees for self-representation." (Soni v. Wellmike Enterprise Co., Ltd. (2014) 224 Cal.App.4th 1477, 1488.) Plaintiff cites no authority supporting any exception to this rule for a situation where a lawyer is acting on behalf of a liquidating law partnership. Laurel SPE's motion for sanctions is denied. Plaintiff's motion to compel was not made in bad faith or without substantial justification, and Laurel SPE has not shown that one or more of the requirements of the subpoena was oppressive. *** Tentative Ruling continues in Part 3 of 3 ***

rulings.Case Title: QIAN & NEMECEK LLP, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED LIABILITY VS. PETER M. RADIN JR.

rulings.Rulings: Plaintiff Qian & Nemecek LLP's Motion To Compel Compliance By Laurel Spe, LLC With Subpoena Duces Tecum And For Sanctions In The Amount Of $10,365.41. *** Tentative Ruling - Part 3 of 3 *** Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: QIAN & NEMECEK LLP, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED LIABILITY VS. PETER M. RADIN JR.
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Compliance By Laurel Spe, Llc With Subpoena Duces Tecum And For Sanctions In The Amount Of $10,365.41
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, April 23, 2019, line 1. *** Tentative Ruling - Part 1 of 3 *** PLAINTIFF QIAN & NEMECEK LLP'S Motion To Compel Compliance By Laurel Spe, Llc With Subpoena Duces Tecum And For Sanctions In The Amount Of $10,365.41. Plaintiff Qian & Nemecek LLP's motion to compel compliance by Laurel SPE, LLC with subpoena duces tecum is granted. Plaintiff's motion for sanctions in the amount of $10,365.41 is denied. Nonparty Laurel SPE, LLC's motion for sanctions is denied. Finally, the parties are directed to meet and confer regarding any objections that Laurel SPE has to the scope of the subpoena, and Laurel SPE is ordered to produce responsive documents within 10 days of the Court's order. On December 14, 2018, Plaintiff served a subpoena duces tecum on non-party Laurel SPE LLC through Anne Conley, its registered agent for service of process, who consented to service of the subpoena by email. (Nemecek Decl. 10-15 & Exs. H, K, L.) The subpoena was a "records and testimony" subpoena rather than a "business records" subpoena; that is, it called for Laurel SPE both to appear in person and to produce the records described in the attachment on January 3, 2019. Laurel SPE did not produce a witness for examination or produce any documents in response to the subpoena by January 3, 2019, the return date on the subpoena, or on any later date. Laurel SPE contends that the subpoena was defective in several respects. Its objections are meritless. First, it contends that the subpoena was invalid because Plaintiffs failed to comply properly to serve Defendant Peter M. Radin, Jr. in accordance with the requirements of Code of Civil Procedure section 1987.5. However, the proof of service filed with the Court on December 14, 2018, of which the Court takes judicial notice, reflects that Mr. Radin was served at his business address. That is the same address at which Mr. Radin has been served with other post-judgment papers-and the same address at which Mr. Radin specifically requested to receive courtesy copies of e-served documents, as set forth in this Court's March 12, 2019 Order. Although Mr. Radin claims not to have received documents previously served on him in this case, he acknowledges in his declaration that he received Plaintiff's papers regarding Laurel SPE. (Radin Decl. 13.) The Court will not tolerate this sort of gamesmanship. *** Tentative Ruling continues in Part 2 of 3 *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ANDRIY KRAVCHUK VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants City And County Of San Francisco, Omar Masry'S Motion For Summary Judgment (Added To Calendar For Purposes Of Entering Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Defendants City And County Of San Francisco, Omar Masry's Motion For Summary Judgment. *** Tentative Ruling - Part 2 of 2 *** Here, the only admissible evidence of the alleged statement is that in a conversation with Ms. Durbin, Mr. Masry "mentioned a possibility of involvement of something 'starting with an M and ending with an L.'" (Sims. Decl., Ex. D [Mar. 16, 2017 email from Durbin to Slobodianiuk].) That statement is ambiguous, and is too vague to be actionable. The term "money laundering" does not "end with an L," nor do Plaintiffs provide evidence of extrinsic circumstances which show that Ms. Durbin understood it in that derogatory sense. It is undisputed that Mr. Masry never referred to money laundering, and that Ms. Durbin did not understand the statement as alleging that Plaintiffs were involved in that crime. (Durbin Decl. 7-10.) Plaintiffs have not supplied any of the deposition testimony referred to in their separate statement, and their declarations are largely inadmissible and therefore insufficient to give rise to a triable issue of fact. (See Cornell v. Berkeley Tennis Club (2017) 18 Cal.App.5th 908, 950-951 [in ruling on defamation claim, trial court properly excluded as hearsay plaintiff's statements about what third parties said they were told]; see generally Bozzi v. Nordstrom, Inc. (2012) 186 Cal.App.4th 755, 761 [only admissible evidence in opposing declarations is liberally construed in deciding whether there is a triable issue].) Further, even if the statement was reasonably susceptible of the defamatory meaning that Plaintiffs draw from it, given the use of the word "possibly," "it appears very doubtful that such a statement properly could be viewed as a statement of fact (which could support a defamation action), rather than an expression of opinion (which cannot)." (Taus v. Loftus (2007) 40 Cal.4th 683, 720; see Baker v. Los Angeles Herald Examiner (1986) 42 Cal.3d 254, 261-262 [reasonable person would understand a statement beginning with the phrase, "[m]y impression is . . ." as a statement of opinion rather than fact]; see also Wynn v. Chanos (N.D. Cal. 2014) 75 F.Supp.3d 1228, 1237-1238 [statements regarding speaker's concerns about casinos' questionable business methods did not reasonably imply a statement that casino operator violated the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act and were statements of opinion].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Defendants are required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. *** End of Tentative Ruling *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ANDRIY KRAVCHUK VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Notice Of Defendants City And County Of San Francisco And Omar Masrys Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, April 23, 2019, line 2. DEFENDANTS CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO, OMAR MASRY'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. *** Tentative Ruling - Part 1 of 2 *** Defendant City and County of San Francisco and Omar Masry's motion for summary judgment is granted. The alleged statement that Plaintiffs were possibly involved with something "starting with an M and ending with an L" is too vague to be actionable as slander. Slander, which is a species of defamation, is a false and unprivileged publication of a statement of fact, including charging a person with a crime. (Civ. Code §§ 44, 46(1).) "Publication means communication to some third person who understands the defamatory meaning of the statement and its application to the person to whom reference is made." (Smith v. Maldonado (1999) 72 Cal.App.4th 637, 645.) "Where the words or other matters which are the subject of a defamation action are of ambiguous meaning, or innocent on their face and defamatory only in the light of extrinsic circumstances, the plaintiff must plead and prove that as used, the words had a particular meaning, or 'innuendo,' which makes them defamatory. [Citations.] Where the language at issue is ambiguous, the plaintiff must also allege the extrinsic circumstances which show the third person reasonably understood it in its derogatory sense (the 'inducement')." (Id. at 645-646.) "The question whether a statement is reasonably susceptible to a defamatory interpretation is a question of law for the trial court. Only once the court has determined that a statement is reasonably susceptible to such a defamatory interpretation does it become a question for the trier of fact whether or not it was so understood." (Id. at 647.) *** Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 2 of 2 *** =(302/EPS)

Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: PLAINTIFF'S MOTION FOR A PROTECTIVE ORDER AS TO THE DEPOSITION OF PLAINTIFF AND CROSS-DEFENDANT SCHEDULED FOR APRIL 17, 2019 AND THE WRITTEN DISCOVEY SERVED ON HER ON MARCH 22, 2019 IS DENIED, EXCEPT TO THE EXTENT THAT THE COURT WILL EXTEND PLAINTIFF'S TIME FOR FILING RESPONSES TO THE DEFENDANTS' DISCOVERY REQUESTS TO TEN (10) DAYS AFTER THE COURT RULES ON PLAINTIFF'S MOTIONS PRESENTLY SET FOR MAY 3, 2019 IF THE COURT DENIES PLAINTIFF'S MOTIONS. PLAINTIFF SOLE BASIS FOR SEEKING A PROTECTIVE ORDER WAS THE PRIOR INAPPROPRIATE CONDUCT OF DEFENDANTS IN RESPONDING TO PLAINTIFF'S DISCOVERY EFFORTS. PLAINTIFF HAS FILED MOTIONS SEEKING TO HAVE THE COURT IMPOSE TEMINATION SANCTIONS FOR THIS ALLEGED MISCONDUCT WHICH IS THE APPROPRAITE REMEDY TO BE SOUGHT AS WELL AS THE IMPOSITION OF MONETARY SANCTIONS. HOWEVER, THERE IS NOTHING IN THE CODE WHICH EMPOWERS THE COURT TO STRIKE PROPER DISCOVERY REQUESTS AS PUNISHMENT FOR DEFENDANTS' PRIOR MISCONDUCT. COUNSEL FOR DEFENDANTS SHOULD PREPARE AN APPROPRIATE ORDER, SUBMIT IT TO COUNSEL FOR PLAINTIFF FOR APPROVAL AS TO FORM AND DELIVER A COPY TO THE COURT FOR SIGNING AT THE HEARING ON APRIL 23, 2019. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rennepar85@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ANDREY KUKUSHKIN VS. MED THRIVE COOPERATIVE, INC. DBA MEDITHRIVE, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, April 23, 2019, line 4. DEFENDANTS MED THRIVE COOPERATIVE, INC., MISHA BREYBURG, SASHA PLOTITSA'S DEMURRER TO 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendants Med Thrive Cooperative, Inc., Misha Breyburg, and Sasha Plotitsa's demurrer to the fourth cause of action for unjust enrichment is sustained with leave to amend. (See Code Civ. Proc. § 430.10(e).) Plaintiff has leave to plead specific facts regarding why the Management Services Agreement may be unenforceable, e.g., if the agreement is found to conflict with the Federal Controlled Substances Act. (See Klein v. Chevron U.S.A., Inc. (2012) 202 Cal.App.4th 1342, 1389.) Defendants' demurrer to the first through fourth causes of action on grounds of lack of capacity (Code Civ. Proc. § 430.10(b)) is overruled. The court rejects Defendants' argument that Plaintiff cannot prosecute this action because nominal defendant Venture Rebel, Inc. is currently suspended for failing to pay its taxes. "[I]n a stockholders' derivative action, the corporation is forced to be a party because any recovery goes through the corporate channel and thus enhances the stockholders' interest therein. The corporation is not attempting to exercise its rights as a corporation. It is being used as a necessary channel by the shareholders. The books and records of the corporation are in the hands of the mismanaging officers according to plaintiff's complaints and thus the shareholders are not in a position to make a return or compute the franchise tax. In such a case it is not equitable to permit section 23301 of the Revenue & Taxation Code to stand as a shield for protecting allegedly dishonest corporate officials. The corporation is not enjoying any of the privileges accorded to such entities; it is more analogous to the winding up of the business." (Reed v. Norman (1957) 48 Cal.2d 338, 343; see also 9 Witkin, Cal. Proc. 5th Appeal § 79 (5th ed. 2008 & 2019 supp.) ["This rule [providing that a suspended corporation may lose the right to invoke legal remedies] will seldom apply to a stockholder's derivative suit."].) These factors are present in this case. Plaintiff alleges he is a minority shareholder who "has been shut out of the Company" and lacks access to corporate documents. (Third Amended Complaint 6.) In addition, it would be unfair to require Plaintiff-a minority shareholder-to pay all of the corporate taxes to revive the corporation. The court does not find Cohen v. Davis Creek Lumber Co. (1957) 151 Cal.App.2d 420, 427 persuasive, as that Court of Appeal opinion (which has never been followed or cited in California) did not address Reed, which is binding Supreme Court authority. (See Auto Equity Sales, Inc. v. Superior Court (1962) 57 Cal.2d 450, 455 ["Under the doctrine of stare decisis, all tribunals exercising inferior jurisdiction are required to follow decisions of courts exercising superior jurisdiction."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: DONALD R MCCAFFREE ET AL VS. VOLKSWAGEN GROUP OF AMERICA INC. A NEW JERSEY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Volkswagen Group Of America, Inc.S And Anderson Enterprises, Inc.S Motion To Strike
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, April 23, 2019, line 7. DEFENDANTS ANDERSON ENTERPRISES INC.. VOLKSWAGEN GROUP OF AMERICA, INC.'S Motion To Strike. Defendants Anderson Enterprises Inc. and Volkswagen Group of America, Inc.'s motion to strike the punitive damages allegations from the complaint by plaintiffs Donald R. McCaffree and Sara McCaffree is denied. Plaintiffs' claim for fraudulent concealment is supported by allegations that Defendants actively concealed from purchasers that a defect in the vehicle's engine posed a serious safety risk to drivers, which is a sufficient allegation of willful and conscious disregard of the rights or safety of others to support a claim for punitive damages pursuant to Civ. Code § 3294. rulings.Case Title: DONALD R MCCAFFREE ET AL VS. VOLKSWAGEN GROUP OF AMERICA INC. A NEW JERSEY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Anderson Enterprises Inc., Volkswagen Group Of America, Inc.'S Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint. (Added For Purposes Of Entering Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Defendants Anderson Enterprises Inc., Volkswagen Group Of America, Inc.'S Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint. *** Tentative Ruling - Part 2 of 2 *** Plaintiff's causes of action based on affirmative misrepresentations are not alleged with the requisite specificity. The only representation that is alleged is the statement that the engine "apportions the right amount of power off the line as it does in motion. Maybe that's why it was awarded the 'Engine of the Year' for the 1.8-2.0L category, presented by Engine Technology International." (Compl. 172.) This statement does not address the expected life of the engine or claim that it is free from defects. (See Daugherty v. American Honda Motor Co., Inc. (2006) 144 Cal.App.4th 824, 834 ["[t]he complaint fails to identify any representation by Honda that its automobiles had ay characteristic they do not have, or are of a standard or quality they are not."].) Nor does the general assertion that the engine provides the "right amount of power" constitute an actionable statement of fact that can be proven to be wrong. (See Demetriades v. Yelp, Inc. (2014) 228 Cal.App.4th 294, 311 ["'a statement that is quantifiable, that makes a claim as to the "specific or absolute characteristics of a product," may be an actionable statement of fact while a general, subjective claim about a product is non-actionable puffery.'"].) Plaintiffs have made only general and conclusory allegations as to misrepresentations allegedly made at the time of sale of the vehicle. (Compl. 66). Plaintiffs also fail to allege that they relied on the written manuals for maintenance schedules before purchasing the vehicle. (Id.. 41.) Plaintiffs have 20 days leave to amend to cure the defect if they can do so in good faith. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. *** End of Tentative Ruling *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: DONALD R MCCAFFREE ET AL VS. VOLKSWAGEN GROUP OF AMERICA INC. A NEW JERSEY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, April 23, 2019, line 7. DEFENDANTS ANDERSON ENTERPRISES INC., VOLKSWAGEN GROUP OF AMERICA, INC.'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. *** Tentative Ruling - Part 1 of 2 *** Defendants Anderson Enterprises, Inc. and Volkswagen Group of America, Inc.'s demurrer to the complaint by plaintiffs Donald R. McCaffree and Sara McCaffree is overruled as to the fourth cause of action for fraudulent concealment and is sustained with leave to amend as to the fifth and sixth causes of action for intentional and negligent misrepresentation. The complaint alleges with sufficient specificity facts supporting a cause of action for fraudulent concealment. "[A] duty to disclose material safety concerns 'can be actionable in four situations: (1) when the defendant is in a fiduciary relationship with the plaintiff; (2) when the defendant had exclusive knowledge of material facts not known to the plaintiff; (3) when the defendant actively conceals a material fact from the plaintiff; or (4) when the defendant makes partial representations but also suppresses some material fact.'" (Gutierrez v. Carmax Auto Superstores California (2018) 19 Cal.App.5th 1234, 1260.) Further, where a cause of action for fraudulent concealment is predicated on non-disclosure and the duty to disclose arises from defendant's superior and exclusive knowledge of material facts, the general rule requiring specific pleading of affirmative misrepresentations is difficult to apply and the particularity requirement is relaxed. (See Alfaro v. Community Housing Improvement System & Planning Assn., Inc. (2009) 171 Cal.App.4th 1356, 1384.) Here, Plaintiffs have adequately pled a duty to disclose a material safety risk (Compl. 20, 47, 50) based on Defendants' exclusive knowledge (id. 48, 49, 63) and active concealment (id. 51, 67, 68). (See also Baranco v. Ford Motor Company (N.D. Cal. 2018) 294 F.Supp.3d 950, 960-963 [plaintiffs adequately pled fraud claim based on Ford's failure to inform them that vehicles were sold with defective door latch switches]; Ehrlich v. BMW of North America, LLC (C.D. Cal. 2010) 801 F.Supp.2d 908, 918-919 [plaintiff sufficiently alleged that defective windshields in MINI created unreasonable safety risk that would be material to a reasonable consumer, that defect was within BMW's exclusive knowledge, and that BMW actively concealed defect].) Plaintiffs' allegation that they would not have purchased the car, had it not been for the omission to disclose the alleged safety defect relating to the timing chain (Compl. 28), is sufficient to plead justifiable reliance. (Baranco, 294 F.Supp.3d at 967.) *** Tentative Ruling continues in Part 2 of 2 *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: J GARCIA CARRION, SA ET AL VS. VINCENT FRIEND ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, April 23, 2019, line 10. DEFENDANTS VINCENT FRIEND, KATHLEEN FRIEND, FRIEND WINE MARKETING, INC, CORDELINA WINE COMPANY, LLC'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. *** Tentative Ruling - Part 1 of 2 *** Defendants Vincent Friend, Kathleen Friend, Friend Wine Marketing, and Cordelina Wine Company's demurrer to the first amended complaint is overruled as to the first and third causes of action for conversion and unfair competition/unfair business practices and sustained without leave to amend as to the second, fourth, and fifth causes of action for breach of contract, promissory estoppel, and equitable estoppel. The Court grants Defendants' request for judicial notice of the underlying materials in the Court's file, including the arbitrators' partial final award and final award, this Court's July 10, 2018 order granting the petition to confirm that award and judgment entered on the same date, and this Court's December 20, 2018 order sustaining Defendants' demurrer without leave to amend as to the prior action brought against them by Plaintiffs (No. CGC-18-565128). The partial final award in the underlying arbitration awarded JGC with "full rights to the use of the name CIV, USA." That language appears in the context of language rejecting CIV, USA's counterclaim against Plaintiffs Friend and FWM for cyber-squatting "on the grounds that they registered the domain name 'civusa.com' and refuse to transfer it to CIV, USA. But the evidence shows that FWM was a license to use the CIV, USA name, stopped using it after the termination of the relationship, and that JGC never paid the $2 called for in Article 5 of the General Agreement to effectuate a transfer of the rights to use the name back to CIV, USA, Inc. Under the circumstances, and as part of this Partial Final Award, JGC shall have full rights to the use of the name CIV, USA." In context, it is clear that the "circumstances" referred to in the award refers to the severance of the parties' business ties, which necessarily contemplated FWM's relinquishment both of the CIV, USA name and of the related domain name. Further, the statements by Defendants' counsel following the issuance of the partial final award are evidence that Defendants understood the General Agreement and the partial final award in the same way. (See Morey v. Vannucci (1998) 64 Cal.App.4th 904, 912 [in giving effect to the mutual intention of the parties in a contract, courts look to, among other things, "the subsequent conduct of the parties"].) Thus, to the extent that Plaintiffs' claims stem from Defendants' failure to comply with this provision of the award, they could not have been brought in the arbitration, and the award does not have any claim preclusive effect. Plaintiffs' first cause of action for conversion is properly pled. Under California law, intangible property interests such as domain names can be subject to conversion. (Welco Electronics, Inc. v. Mora (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 202, 213; Kremen v. Cohen (9th Cir. 2003) 337 F.3d 1024, 1030, 1036.) The first amended complaint sufficiently alleges the elements of such a claim: (1) a right to ownership of the domain name; (2) Defendants' wrongful conversion of the domain by allowing it to falls into the hands of a third party; and (3) damages suffered by Plaintiffs as a result. (FAC 26-30; Plummer v. Day/Eisenberg, LLC (2010) 184 Cal.App.4th 38, 45.) *** Tentative Ruling continues in Part 2 of 2 *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: J GARCIA CARRION, SA ET AL VS. VINCENT FRIEND ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Vincent Friend, Kathleen Friend, Friend Wine Marketing, Inc, Cordelina Wine Company, Llc Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint (Added To Calendar For Purpose Of Entering Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Defendant Vincent Friend, Kathleen Friend, Friend Wine Marketing, Inc, Cordelina Wine Company, Llc Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint. *** Tentative Ruling - Part 2 of 2 *** The third cause of action for unfair competition/unfair business practices is also properly pled. The Unfair Competition Law is not limited to specific practices and prohibits any "unfair or fraudulent business act or practice." (Bus. & Prof. Code § 17200; Cel-Tech Communications, Inc. v. Los Angeles Cellular Telephone Co. (1999) 20 Cal.4th 163, 180 ["[T]he unfair competition law's scope is broad."].) The first amended complaint alleges that Plaintiffs lost money as a result of unfair conduct by Defendants such as failure to withdraw CIV, USA's brand registrations and transfer the civusa.com domain name to Plaintiffs. (FAC 41-47.) Plaintiffs' second, fourth, and fifth causes of action for breach of contract, promissory estoppel, and equitable estoppel fail because the first amended complaint does not allege any clear promise or offer by Defendants to transfer the domain and other CIV, USA registrations to Plaintiffs. Defense counsel's alleged statements suggest a willingness to transfer to the rights to Plaintiffs, but were not sufficient to constitute a promise that could form the basis for an enforceable contract. (Civ. Code § 1549; FAC 21-23.) Similarly, there is no allegation of a "clear and unambiguous promise" on which Plaintiffs could have reasonably relied for purposes of promissory estoppel. (Joffe v. City of Huntington Park (2011) 201 Cal.App.4th 492, 513.) The first amended complaint does not allege any misrepresentation or concealment, nor does it allege that Plaintiffs were "ignorant of the true facts" to support an equitable estoppel claim. (Banco Mercantil, S.A. v. Sauls, Inc. (1956) 140 Cal.App.2d 316, 323.) Plaintiffs do not allege that Defendants made any affirmative misrepresentation that they would transfer the domain name. Rather, they allege that they asked Defendant to transfer the domain in accordance with their rights, but that Defendants failed to do so. Finally, Plaintiffs' surviving claims are properly pled against Defendants Kathleen Friend and Cordelina Wine Company, as the domain name and brand registrations are alleged to be registered under the name of Ms. Friend, who served as an officer of Cordelina. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. *** End of Tentative Ruling *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: EARLISHA NASH VS. SYNCTRUCK, LLC A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Synctruck, Llc Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration In Lieu Of Filing An Answer (Added For Purpose Of Entering Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Defendant Synctruck, LLC'S Motion To Compel Arbitration In Lieu Of Filing An Answer. *** Tentative Ruling - Part 2 of 2 *** As a result, the FAA does not preempt Labor Code section 229, which prohibits arbitration of claims for "the collection of due and unpaid wages claimed by an individual" brought pursuant to sections 200 through 244 of the Labor Code. (Lab. Code § 229; Lane v. Francis Capital Management (2014) 224 Cal.App.4th 676, 684.) Plaintiff's second, fifth, and eighth causes of action for failure to pay overtime compensation, failure to indemnify necessary business expenses, and unfair business practices are based on statutes outside of the relevant article of the Labor Code. Lane further excludes from the scope of section 229 meal periods penalties (Plaintiff's third cause of action), rest break penalties (fourth cause of action), waiting time penalties (sixth cause of action), and failure to provide accurate itemized wage statements (seventh cause of action). (Id.) Only Plaintiff's first cause of action, to the extent it seeks unpaid wages under Labor Code section 204, may not be arbitrated under section 229. Therefore, that claim must be stayed pending arbitration of the remaining causes of action. In sum, Plaintiff's individual claims brought as the second through eighth causes of action must be arbitrated. Plaintiff's class claims and first individual cause of action are stayed pending the completion of the arbitration. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. *** End of Tentative Ruling *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: EARLISHA NASH VS. SYNCTRUCK, LLC A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration In Lieu Of Filing An Answer
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, April 23, 2019, line 8. DEFENDANT SYNCTRUCK, LLC'S Motion To Compel Arbitration In Lieu Of Filing An Answer. *** Tentative Ruling - Part 1 of 2 *** Defendant Synctruck, LLC's motion to compel arbitration in lieu of filing an answer is granted in large part. Plaintiff Earlisha Nash is seeking to pursue claims both on behalf of a class and on an individual basis. Plaintiff's individual claims undoubtedly fall within the scope of employment and are therefore covered by the arbitration agreement. Based on the plain language of the Arbitration Agreement, there is no agreement to arbitrate class claims, so those claims must remain in this Court. (See Flamenco Decl. Exh. A, 3 ["[T]his agreement applies only to Claims between Employee individually and Employer. The Parties do not agree to arbitrate class actions or representative actions."].) "[A] party may not be compelled under the FAA to submit to class arbitration unless there is a contractual basis for concluding that the party agreed to do so." (Stolt-Nielsen S.A. v. AnimalFeeds International Corp. (2010) 559 U.S. 662, 684-685.) Contrary to Plaintiff's position, there is no ambiguity in the quoted provision of the agreement. The parties' arbitration agreement is subject to the exemption in FAA section 1 for "contracts of employment of . . . any other class of workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce." (9 U.S.C. § 1.) In its recent decision in Nieto v. Fresno Beverage Company, Inc. (2019) 33 Cal.App.5th 274, the court expressly rejected the precise argument that Synctruck makes here: "that because [the employee's] delivery routes did not cross state lines, but were merely intrastate, the exemption cannot apply to him." The court noted at the outset that "delivery truck drivers whose primary purpose is to physically transport interstate goods are consistently held to be transportation workers under section 1 of the FAA." (Id.) While Nieto's deliveries were exclusively to destinations within California, the court held that "a transportation worker does not necessarily have to physically cross state lines in order to engage in the movement of goods in interstate commerce." The court concluded that because Nieto's intrastate deliveries were "essentially the last phase of a continuous journey of the interstate commerce" of the goods he was transporting, he was "engaged in interstate commerce through his participation in the continuation of the movement of interstate goods to their destinations." The same conclusion follows here. Defendant has not adequately distinguished the facts of this case from those in Nieto to compel a different result here. *** Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 2 of 2 *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: KATHLEEN RODANTE VS. WHISTLE LABS INC. A DELAWARE CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO ADMIT COUNSEL PRO HAC VICE
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, April 23, 2019, line 9. DEFENDANT WHISTLE LABS INC'S MOTION TO ADMIT COUNSEL PRO HAC VICE. The verified application of Thomas R. Davies to appear as counsel pro hac vice for Defendant Whistle Labs, Inc. is granted. No opposition filed, and the application complies with CRC 9.40. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
